title: The connection between hip tightness and stored sexual trauma
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
Your hips don't lie. But they do hold secrets. 

 I remember lying on a yoga mat years ago, staring at a ceiling I couldn't see because my eyes were squee...
htmlContent:
Your hips don't lie. But they do hold secrets.

I remember lying on a yoga mat years ago, staring at a ceiling I couldn't see because my eyes were squeezed shut. The instructor said "open your hips" and I felt nothing. Not a stretch. Not a release. Just a dense, immovable wall where my hip flexors should have been. I pushed. I breathed. I tried harder. Nothing budged.

Later that night, I sat on my couch and cried for no reason I could name. My hips ached with a dull throb that felt less like muscle soreness and more like grief.

Here's the thing. That yoga class wasn't the first time my hips refused to cooperate. They'd been locked up for years. Tight hamstrings. Clicking hips. A lower back that seized up any time I tried to run or dance or feel anything resembling joy in my pelvis. I assumed it was posture. Or sitting too much. Or just bad genetics.

I was wrong.

What I didn't know then, what I'm only starting to understand now, is that my hips weren't just tight. They were guarding something. A history. A story my body had been telling in the only language it had left: tension.

Stay with me here. This gets specific. And personal. And maybe a little uncomfortable. But if you've ever wondered why your hips feel like concrete even after years of stretching, I think you'll recognize something in what I'm about to say.

The psoas: your body's emotional muscle

Let's talk about the psoas. It's a deep muscle that connects your spine to your femur. It's the only muscle in the body that connects your upper half to your lower half. And it sits right next to your kidneys, your adrenal glands, and your sacral nerve plexus.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, called the psoas the "muscle of the soul." He wasn't being poetic. He was being literal. The psoas is directly wired into your autonomic nervous system. When you feel threatened, your psoas contracts. It's part of the startle response, part of the freeze response, part of the collapse that happens when fight or flight isn't possible.

Think about what happens when you're scared. You curl forward. Your belly tightens. Your hips tuck under. That's your psoas engaging. Now imagine that response happening over and over, day after day, year after year. The muscle doesn't get the signal to let go. It stays in a state of chronic contraction.

This is where stored trauma comes in. And I'm not talking about big-T trauma only. I'm talking about the small betrayals, the unwanted touches, the times you froze when someone crossed a boundary you didn't know you had. The times you dissociated during sex. The times you pretended to enjoy something because it was easier than saying no.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, spent decades documenting how trauma gets trapped in the body. His research shows that traumatic experiences aren't just memories stored in the brain. They're physiological events stored in tissues, in fascia, in the deep layers of muscle that most stretching can't reach.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk walks through the science in a way that doesn't feel clinical or detached. He explains why your body reacts the way it does. And he offers real pathways for recovery that don't involve just talking about what happened.

But here's what van der Kolk doesn't say explicitly, though it's implied throughout his work: the hips are ground zero for sexual trauma. The psoas wraps around the pelvic bowl. It attaches to the lumbar spine. It's intimately connected to the sacrum, which is the bony plate at the base of your spine that houses your sacral nerves. Those nerves control pelvic function, sexual response, and bowel and bladder function. The same nerves that get hijacked during sexual violation.

So when someone says "my hips are tight," what they might be saying, without knowing it, is "my pelvis is still bracing against something that happened years ago."

The freeze response and the locked pelvis

Let's get specific about what sexual trauma does to the body during the event itself. Because this isn't abstract. This is physiological.

When a person experiences unwanted sexual contact, the nervous system has three options: fight, flight, or freeze. In many cases, especially with children or in situations where resistance would escalate danger, the body chooses freeze. It's a survival strategy. The body goes limp. The muscles contract in a specific pattern to immobilize. The psoas locks down. The pelvic floor tightens. The diaphragm stops moving freely.

This is called tonic immobility. It's the same response that possums show when they play dead. And it's incredibly common in sexual trauma survivors.

Here's the problem. The freeze response is designed to be temporary. Once the threat passes, the body should release. It should shake. It should tremble. It should discharge the stored energy. But in trauma, especially repeated trauma, the body never gets that release signal. The freeze becomes chronic. The psoas stays locked. The pelvis stays braced.

Years later, you're in a yoga class wondering why you can't touch your toes. Or you're in a massage wondering why your hips feel like granite. Or you're in bed with a partner you trust, and your body just won't open. And you think something's wrong with you.

Nothing's wrong with you. Your body is doing exactly what it learned to do to survive.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in sensorimotor psychotherapy, talks about this extensively. She emphasizes that the body holds procedural memories, which are memories of how to do things, like how to brace for impact or how to stay still during danger. These memories don't require conscious recall. They're encoded in muscle tension, in breath patterns, in postural habits. You don't have to remember the trauma for your hips to remember it.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. It breaks down the freeze response in detail and offers gentle exercises for releasing trapped survival energy. It's not about reliving trauma. It's about helping the body complete the responses it couldn't finish at the time.

I'll be honest with you. Reading this stuff made me angry at first. Angry at every yoga teacher who told me to "breathe into my hips" without understanding what they were asking. Angry at every fitness influencer who said tight hips were just a flexibility problem. Angry at myself for not knowing sooner.

But anger is useful. It tells you something matters. And this matters.

Why stretching alone won't fix it

I spent years stretching my hips. Pigeon pose. Frog pose. Happy baby. Lizard pose. I bought foam rollers and lacrosse balls and massage guns. I went to yin yoga classes where we held poses for five minutes at a time. And every time, my hips would soften slightly during the stretch, then snap right back to tight within an hour.

That's because stretching is a mechanical intervention for a nervous system problem. You can stretch a muscle all day, but if the nervous system is still sending contract signals, the muscle will just contract again. It's like trying to calm a screaming child by taping their mouth shut. You're addressing the symptom, not the cause.

What actually helps is nervous system regulation. And that's a slower, more subtle process.

Deb Dana, a clinician who specializes in polyvagal theory, describes the nervous system as a ladder. At the top is ventral vagal, the state of safety and connection. In the middle is sympathetic, the fight-or-flight state. At the bottom is dorsal vagal, the freeze or collapse state. For trauma survivors, the nervous system often gets stuck in the bottom two rungs. And from those states, the body doesn't feel safe enough to release chronic tension.

So the work isn't about forcing the hips open. The work is about convincing the nervous system that it's safe to let go. That takes time. That takes gentleness. That takes learning to notice what your body is doing without trying to fix it.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). I mention this because hypervigilance, a common symptom of trauma, often involves auditory sensitivity. When your nervous system is constantly scanning for threat, loud or unexpected sounds can keep you in a state of high alert. Reducing that input can help your body feel safe enough to begin releasing. It sounds small. It's not.

I also want to mention Gabor Mate, the physician who wrote about the connection between stress, trauma, and illness. A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Mate's work helped me understand that my hip tightness wasn't isolated. It was part of a larger pattern of bodily suppression that included chronic fatigue, digestive issues, and a tendency to ignore my own needs. The hips were just the most visible symptom.

What recovery actually looks like

I don't want to give you a neat five-step plan. Recovery from stored trauma isn't linear. It's messy. It's two steps forward and three steps back. It's crying in child's pose. It's having a panic attack during a massage. It's realizing that some parts of your body might never fully release, and that's okay.

What I can tell you is what's helped me, and what I see helping others.

First, stop forcing. If a stretch or exercise makes your body tighten more, stop doing it. Your body isn't being stubborn. It's being protective. Honor that protection. Thank it. Then find a gentler approach.

Second, work with your breath. But not the deep belly breathing that's often prescribed. For many trauma survivors, deep breathing into the belly can feel threatening because it brings awareness to a part of the body that's associated with violation. Instead, try gentle, shallow breaths. Or just notice your natural breath without changing it. Let the nervous system lead, not your willpower.

Third, consider body-based therapy. Somatic Experiencing, sensorimotor psychotherapy, and EMDR all work with the body's stored trauma in ways that talk therapy alone can't. Janina Fisher, who integrates Internal Family Systems with trauma treatment, offers excellent resources for understanding how different parts of you hold different pieces of the story.

Fourth, be patient with the process. I'm years into this work, and my hips still get tight when I'm stressed. The difference is that now I know what it means. I don't fight it. I don't panic. I just notice. I say, "Oh, there you are. I see you. What do you need?" And sometimes the answer is nothing. Sometimes the answer is a gentle rock. Sometimes the answer is a walk. Sometimes the answer is just being witnessed.

Your hips might be tight for reasons you don't fully understand yet. That's not a failure. That's information. And information, even painful information, is the first step toward something different.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did your hips shift? Did your breath change? Did you feel a flicker of something, recognition or resistance or maybe both? That's your body talking. It's been talking all along. You just weren't trained to listen.

You can learn. I'm still learning. And that's enough.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's essential guide to understanding how trauma lives in the body
  • Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link) - A simple tool for reducing sensory overwhelm and supporting nervous system regulation
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's foundational work on healing trauma through the body
  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - Gabor Mate's exploration of how stress and hidden trauma contribute to chronic illness
